children.name: Rana
children.description: The archetypal frogs of the Northern Hemisphere — agile, long-legged leapers of ponds, ditches, and damp grassland. The European Common Frog is one of the first signs of spring across northern Europe, gathering in ponds in late February and March to breed in vast, croaking congregations. Its spawn — masses of black-centred, gel-coated eggs — is one of the most recognisable sights of early spring across Britain and Scandinavia. Rana frogs are sensitive indicators of environmental quality: the Moor Frog's deep-blue breeding males are unmistakable, and the Agile Frog is a reliable indicator of ancient, undisturbed habitat.

children.name: Rana temporaria
children.description: The most familiar amphibian in Britain and much of northern Europe. Breeds earlier than almost any other frog — pond ice may still be present when the first adults arrive to spawn. The adult's colour is highly variable: brown, grey, olive, or even red.

children.name: Rana arvalis
children.description: During a brief breeding window of a few days, males turn an intense cobalt blue — one of the most striking colour transformations in European herpetology. A boreal and steppe species, common in Scandinavia and Russia.

children.name: Rana dalmatina
children.description: One of Europe's most athletic frogs, capable of leaping over 2 metres. Associated with ancient deciduous woodland. A very early breeder — spawns before the Common Frog in some regions. Declining across its range.

children.name: Rana sylvatica
children.description: The only frog found north of the Arctic Circle in North America. Survives freezing solid in winter — up to 65% of body water converts to ice around (not inside) cells. Heart stops completely; body temperature may reach -8°C. Thaws and walks away in spring.

children.name: Rana pyrenaica
children.description: A small, agile brown frog endemic to fast-flowing mountain streams of the Pyrenees on both the French and Spanish sides. Restricted range makes it vulnerable; listed as Near Threatened by the IUCN.

children.name: Rana iberica
children.description: A slender stream-dwelling brown frog endemic to the northwestern Iberian Peninsula. Tied to cool, clean montane waters and assessed as Near Threatened due to habitat loss.

children.name: Rana latastei
children.description: A reddish-brown lowland frog restricted to floodplain woodlands of the Po Valley and adjacent regions. Listed as Vulnerable owing to drainage and loss of riparian forest.

children.name: Rana italica
children.description: A small brown frog endemic to the Apennine streams of peninsular Italy, where it clings to rocks in cold running water.

children.name: Rana graeca
children.description: A brown stream frog endemic to the Balkan Peninsula, inhabiting rocky mountain streams from Greece through the former Yugoslavia. Listed as Near Threatened due to water abstraction and pollution of its stream habitats.

children.name: Rana japonica
children.description: A widespread brown frog of Japan's lowland rice paddies and woodland edges, breeding in winter and early spring in still water.

children.name: Rana ornativentris
children.description: A Japanese brown frog of cooler hill and mountain habitats, distinguished by mottled flanks and a marbled belly.

children.name: Rana chensinensis
children.description: A brown frog of northern China harvested for the traditional medicinal product 'hasma'; widespread but locally pressured by collection.

children.name: Rana macrocnemis
children.description: A variable brown frog of the Caucasus, Anatolia, and the Iranian highlands, occupying montane meadows and forest streams. Several subspecies are recognized across its wide and fragmented range.

children.name: Rana camerani
children.description: A brown frog endemic to the Talysh Mountains of Azerbaijan and northern Iran, inhabiting humid forest streams at moderate elevations. Known from a restricted range and considered Near Threatened.

children.name: Rana asiatica
children.description: A brown frog of Central Asian mountain systems from Kazakhstan through Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan, breeding in cold streams and alpine lakes. Populations are fragmented across isolated mountain ranges.

children.name: Rana dybowskii
children.description: A large brown frog of the Russian Far East, northeastern China, and Korea, found in broadleaf forests near streams and ponds. Named for the Polish naturalist Benedykt Dybowski who explored Siberia.

children.name: Rana amurensis
children.description: A cold-adapted brown frog ranging across Siberia to Sakhalin and northern Mongolia, one of the few amphibians surviving in permafrost regions. Overwinters underwater in oxygen-rich streams beneath the ice.

children.name: Rana rugosa
children.description: A small, rough-skinned frog of Japan and Korea, found on damp rocks near streams and in irrigated farmland. The warty dorsal skin with scattered tubercles gives it its common name.

children.name: Rana okinavana
children.description: A brown frog endemic to the Ryukyu Islands of Japan, inhabiting forest streams on Okinawa and Amami-Oshima. Threatened by habitat loss from development and invasive predators.

children.name: Rana sakuraii
children.description: A stream-breeding brown frog endemic to central Honshu, Japan, found along clear mountain rivulets in deciduous forest. Males call from beneath rocks in early spring.

children.name: Rana pirica
children.description: The common brown frog of Hokkaido, Japan, breeding in ponds and marshes from lowlands to subalpine zones. Closely related to R. dybowskii of the continental Far East.

children.name: Rana tagoi
children.description: A brown frog endemic to Honshu, Shikoku, and Kyushu in Japan, breeding in small streams and seepage areas in hill forests. Males produce a distinctive high-pitched call from under rocks.

children.name: Rana tsushimensis
children.description: A brown frog endemic to Tsushima Island between Japan and Korea, inhabiting forested valleys and paddy margins. Its restricted island range makes it vulnerable to habitat modification.

children.name: Rana longicrus
children.description: A brown frog of southern China and northern Vietnam distinguished by its unusually long hind limbs. Found in montane forests and rice terraces at moderate elevations.

children.name: Rana huanrensis
children.description: A brown frog endemic to northeastern China and adjacent parts of Korea, inhabiting broadleaf forests near cold streams. Harvested locally alongside R. chensinensis for traditional medicine.

children.name: Pelophylax
children.description: Water frogs — the large, aquatic, green frogs of Europe and western Asia. They spend far more time in water than the brown frogs of the Rana group, and their loud, persistent croaking from the margins of ponds, lakes, and slow rivers is one of the defining sounds of a European summer. The Edible Frog is a stable hybrid between Pool Frog and Marsh Frog that reproduces by a process called hybridogenesis — replacing its own genome with the genome of one parent species each generation in an unusual form of genetic parasitism.

children.name: Pelophylax ridibundus
children.description: Europe's largest native frog. Introduced to Britain in the 1930s from Hungary; now well established in Romney Marsh and other wetlands. Highly vocal — croaking begins in April and continues through summer. Feeds on invertebrates, small fish, and occasionally other frogs.

children.name: Pelophylax lessonae
children.description: Native to Britain but extinct by the 1990s due to habitat loss; reintroduced from Scandinavian stock starting in 2005. The Pool Frog is now a Biodiversity Action Plan species, representing one of the few cases of a recently extinct British vertebrate being reintroduced.

children.name: Pelophylax esculentus
children.description: A stable hybrid of P. lessonae × P. ridibundus that reproduces by hybridogenesis — discarding one parental genome each generation. The origin of the 'cuisses de grenouilles' of French cuisine. Classified as a protected species across much of Europe.

children.name: Pelophylax perezi
children.description: The common green water frog of the Iberian Peninsula and parts of North Africa, abundant around ponds, canals, and slow rivers.

children.name: Pelophylax bergeri
children.description: A green water frog of the Italian Peninsula and Corsica, part of the hybridogenetic water-frog complex.

children.name: Pelophylax saharicus
children.description: A robust green frog of North African oases, streams, and irrigation channels from Morocco to Egypt, tolerant of warm and brackish water.

children.name: Pelophylax nigromaculatus
children.description: A common East Asian pond frog of rice paddies in China, Korea, and Japan, marked with bold dark dorsal blotches.

children.name: Pelophylax porosus
children.description: A widespread water frog of Japan's main islands, breeding in rice paddies, ponds, and slow streams from lowlands to hills. More brownish than European Pelophylax, with a dark mid-dorsal stripe.

children.name: Pelophylax plancyi
children.description: A green water frog of eastern China with bright yellow dorsolateral folds, common in lowland ponds and marshes. One of the most abundant frogs in the Yangtze basin.

children.name: Pelophylax chosenicus
children.description: A water frog endemic to the Korean Peninsula, inhabiting rice paddies and irrigation ditches in lowland agricultural landscapes. Threatened by pesticide use and wetland drainage.

children.name: Pelophylax lateralis
children.description: A green pond frog of Central Asia from Uzbekistan to western China, found in oases, irrigation canals, and lake margins. Tolerant of warm, stagnant water in arid landscapes.

children.name: Lithobates
children.description: American true frogs — the diverse radiation of Ranidae in the New World. The American Bullfrog is the largest frog in North America and one of the world's most damaging invasive species, introduced worldwide for frog-leg production; it consumes virtually anything that fits in its mouth, carries chytrid fungus (*Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis*) without succumbing to it, and has contributed to the extinction or severe decline of native amphibians on every continent where it has been introduced.

children.name: Lithobates catesbeianus
children.description: Listed among the world's 100 worst invasive species. The deep, resonant 'jug-o-rum' call of males carries hundreds of metres. Males defend territories in ponds and may eat ducklings, small snakes, other frogs, and even bats. A carrier of the amphibian-killing chytrid fungus.

children.name: Lithobates pipiens
children.description: One of the most widespread frogs in North America and formerly the most commonly used animal in biology education. Populations declined sharply in the 1970s–80s due to disease, UV exposure through ozone depletion, and habitat loss.

children.name: Lithobates clamitans
children.description: A common, semi-aquatic frog of eastern North America, found in virtually any permanent water body from ponds to urban parks. Males produce a single banjo-string-like twang during the breeding season.

children.name: Lithobates sphenocephalus
children.description: A slim spotted frog of the southeastern United States, common in wetlands, ditches, and floodplain pools.

children.name: Lithobates grylio
children.description: A large aquatic frog of the southeastern US whose call resembles a pig's grunt; harvested for frog legs in Florida.

children.name: Lithobates heckscheri
children.description: A dark, large frog of swampy rivers and cypress backwaters in the southeastern United States.

children.name: Lithobates areolatus
children.description: A stout, secretive frog of the central US that shelters in crayfish burrows; declining and of conservation concern across much of its range.

children.name: Lithobates chiricahuensis
children.description: A spotted frog of mountain wetlands in Arizona, New Mexico, and northern Mexico, federally listed as Threatened in the US due to disease and habitat loss.

children.name: Lithobates septentrionalis
children.description: A northern frog of boreal lakes and ponds from New England to Alaska, named for its musky odour reminiscent of mink. The most cold-tolerant of the North American ranids, breeding in late summer.

children.name: Lithobates palustris
children.description: A slender frog of cool, clear springs and bogs in eastern North America, distinguished by rectangular dark spots arranged in two rows. Skin secretions are mildly toxic, deterring some predators.

children.name: Lithobates virgatipes
children.description: A small frog of Atlantic coastal plain wetlands from New Jersey to Florida, found in sphagnum bogs and seepage slopes. Declining in the northern part of its range due to wetland drainage.

children.name: Lithobates capito
children.description: A large-headed frog of the southeastern US sandhill and longleaf pine ecosystems, dependent on gopher tortoise burrows for shelter. Listed as Near Threatened due to fire suppression and loss of its commensal host.

children.name: Lithobates blairi
children.description: A prairie frog of the Great Plains from Canada to Texas, breeding in temporary rain pools and buffalo wallows. Formerly considered a subspecies of L. pipiens but now recognized as distinct.

children.name: Lithobates forreri
children.description: A large leopard frog of western Mexico and the southwestern United States, inhabiting streams and ponds in arid and semi-arid regions. One of the largest ranids in North America.

children.name: Lithobates berlandieri
children.description: A leopard frog of the Rio Grande basin from Colorado through Texas into northeastern Mexico, common in irrigation ditches and stock tanks. Tolerant of alkaline and warm waters.

children.name: Lithobates palmipes
children.description: A large aquatic frog from the Amazon and Orinoco basins.

children.name: Odorrana
children.description: Odorous frogs — a diverse East Asian genus of stream-breeding frogs, many species restricted to single islands or mountain ranges. Males often call from rocks beside cascading water, and several species emit a distinctive musky odour when handled.

children.name: Odorrana amamiensis

children.name: Odorrana splendida

children.name: Odorrana odorata

children.name: Amolops
children.description: Cascade frogs — torrent specialists of Asian mountain streams, equipped with ventral suckers on their toes that grip wet rock in rushing water. Many species are restricted to single mountain ranges or watersheds.

children.name: Amolops ricketti
children.description: A torrent frog of southeastern China and northern Vietnam, clinging to spray-splashed rocks beside waterfalls. Tadpoles possess a large oral sucker to resist being swept away.

children.name: Amolops mantzorum
children.description: A large cascade frog of western China's Sichuan and Shaanxi provinces, inhabiting cold mountain torrents in temperate forest. Tadpoles overwinter under ice in fast-flowing water.

children.name: Amolops hongkongensis
children.description: A torrent frog endemic to Hong Kong and adjacent Guangdong, found on boulders in shaded hill streams. One of the few amphibians adapted to Hong Kong's urban-adjacent forest remnants.

children.name: Amolops daiyunensis

children.name: Amolops chunganensis

children.name: Amolops loudonii

children.name: Amolops marmoratus

children.name: Nidirana

children.name: Staurois
children.description: Bornean rock frogs — small, agile torrent specialists of Borneo and the Philippines, with enlarged toe discs for gripping wet rock. Males perform foot-flagging displays to communicate over the noise of rushing water.

children.name: Staurois tuberilingus
